edge: Deep image prior (DIP) formulation includes the regularization parameter (λ) as a key component for handling image restoration (IR) and visual inverse problems.
Deep image prior (DIP) focuses on image restoration (IR) problems and has been successful in tackling various image restoration tasks, such as denoising, super-resolution, and inpainting.
The optimization process of Deep image prior (DIP) is often slow, which has led to the need for modifications to speed it up for practical usage in time-sensitive applications.
The optimization process of Deep image prior (DIP) is crucial for achieving high-quality image restoration results in tasks such as denoising, super-resolution, and inpainting.
Deep image prior (DIP) benefits from reintroducing explicit priors, such as the sparse gradient prior encoded by total-variation regularization, to enhance its performance in image restoration tasks.
The proposed method outperforms MetaDIP, which uses meta-learning for good initializers with extra data, by achieving competitive restoration quality in a minimal amount of time.
Reducing the network depth is one of the crucial modifications proposed for Deep image prior (DIP) to achieve a substantial speedup in the optimization process for image restoration tasks.
Image restoration (IR) involves tasks such as image denoising, super-resolution, and image inpainting.
A visual object of interest is parameterized as x = Gθ(z), where Gθ is a DNN and z is a seed.
A visual inverse problem is represented as y ≈ f (x), where y is the observation and f models the observation process.
The observation process is modeled by function f in the context of a visual inverse problem.
A salient feature of DIP for image restoration is that it is training-free, meaning no extra data is needed for problem-solving.
In practice, the DNN Gθ in DIP is often a structured convolutional neural network.
A structured convolutional neural network is significantly overparameterized.
Due to heavy overparametrization, overfitting can occur leading to fitting noise in addition to desired visual content.
When overfitting occurs, the recovered image x accounts for potential noise in y along with desired visual content.
The learning process primarily picks up the desired visual content before fitting the noise.
Overparametrization combined with convolutional structures in Gθ leads to an implicit regularization effect.
Convolutional structures in Gθ along with first-order optimization methods contribute to the regularization effect.
Locating the peak-performance point and stopping at the fitting sharp can provide a good estimate for x.
DIP has shown remarkable empirical success in solving image reconstructions.
Comparison between DIP and DRP-DIP for image denoising shows differences in immediate reconstructions and performance peak time.

Overfitting is a challenge faced in the practical deployment of DIP, where previous works have set the number of iterations directly based on visual inspection and trial-and-error. It shows the potential of DIP but does not provide an operational ES strategy.
Visual inspection is mentioned as a method for setting the number of iterations in DIP, however, it precludes large-scale batch processing or deployment into unfamiliar scenarios.
Trial-and-error is mentioned as another method for setting the number of iterations in DIP, where tuning is performed with reference to the unknown groundtruth object.

Ideas to mitigate overfitting in DIP include controlling the capacity of Gθ, explicit regularization, and explicit noise modeling, which tend to prolong the iteration process and push peak performance to final iterations.

The slowness of DIP in reaching the performance peak from random initialization is highlighted, where each step entails a forward and backward pass through Gθ and is much more expensive than typical iterative methods.
The optimization slowness of DIP is mentioned as taking up to tens of minutes to hours on a state-of-the-art GPU card like Nvidia V100, hindering its applicability to time-sensitive tasks.
The optimization slowness of DIP inevitably hinders its applicability to time-sensitive problems.
Mitigating overfitting escalates the slowness of DIP.
DRP is a variant proposed to tackle the slowness issue of DIP.
DRP optimizes the DIP seed while freezing randomly-initialized network weights.
DRP reduces the network depth compared to DIP.
DRP includes additional explicit prior(s) for regularization, such as total variation (TV) regularization.
DRP is shown to be much more efficient than meta-learning-based meta-DIP for speedup.
ES method in [30] can be directly integrated, without modification, to detect near-peak performance for DRP.
ES method in [30] can provide a complete solution to both overfitting and slowness issues in DIP.
The ES method in [30] provides a complete solution to both overfitting and slowness issues in DIP.
Meta-leaning has been used for speedup by learning good initializers and learning rates.
DRP needs zero extra data for speedup, inheriting DIP's advantages.
Meta-learning-based initializers do not have speed advantage overall compared to DRP.

Optimizing the input seed is much faster than optimizing the network weights for image regression. Therefore, freezing network weights and optimizing the input seed is a better choice.

While one may argue that directly taking a pretrained DNN for initialization can be another option, as we have discussed in Sec. 2, taking weights from a pretrained DNN has several foreseeable limitations.
Freezing network weights and merely optimizing the input seed z leads to better results in image regression.
Randomly setting the initial weights for Gθ is much better than directly taking weights from a pretrained model.

Cutting down the depth is expected to reduce the non-linearity of the final optimization loss
Reducing the depth is expected to reduce the non-linearity of the final optimization loss, making it more smooth and potentially speeding up convergence.
Different depths of the backbone neural network are explored for image denoising, affecting the restoration quality.
The shallower the backbone neural network, the less the OPT time, which is a desirable property for the model.
Using a shallow backbone network has a detrimental impact on the restoration quality, while reducing the OPT time.
A question arises on how to bring back restoration quality while still benefiting from shallow neural networks as backbone networks.
The trade-off between restoration quality and OPT time is discussed, as the shallow backbone network reduces OPT time but impacts restoration quality.
The degradation in restoration quality may be due to the optimization process suppressing implicit priors, which leads to a discussion on reintroducing explicit priors.
Gθ dramatically suppresses the implicit prior induced by DIP’s implicit regularization. To bring back the restoration quality, additional explicit priors are proposed.

Adding TV to DRP significantly boosts restoration quality, as shown by higher PSNR. This observation holds across different noise types and levels.
